nativeRange.habitat: The Payara inhabits fast-flowing, well-oxygenated river channels, rapids, and the mouths of tributary streams across the Amazon and Orinoco drainages. It is found primarily in the mid-water to surface zone of main river channels and rocky areas with strong current.

decorPreferences: minimal decor to maximize open swimming space
smooth rounded boulders or large river-worn stones
robust driftwood for structure without obstruction
strong directional flow from powerful filtration or powerheads
very tightly sealed lid — powerful jumper

diet.notes: The Payara is an obligate piscivore (a fish-eater) in the wild, feeding primarily on other fish — including piranhas — using a pair of elongated lower-jaw fangs that can reach around 6 cm in length to impale prey. In captivity, weaning onto non-live food is extremely difficult, and most specimens will only reliably accept live feeder fish over the long term. Some individuals can eventually be transitioned to large chunks of fresh whole fish such as smelt, whitebait, or tilapia pieces, large earthworms, and occasionally frozen whole fish. Prepared pellets and sticks are rarely accepted. Vitamin supplementation is advisable if the diet consists entirely of feeder fish. Using gut-loaded or quarantined feeder fish reduces the risk of introducing disease.

goodWith: large, robust, similarly-sized fish too large to be eaten (as tankmates only in very large public-aquarium setups)
large doradid catfish (bottom dwellers that stay out of mid-water)
large armored catfish (Pseudoplatystoma, Brachyplatystoma) in specialist setups
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be eaten (effectively all community fish)
piranhas and other aggressive fish at similar size (mutual predation or injury)
conspecifics except in very large groups in public-aquarium scale tanks
any fish without fast-flowing, high-oxygen conditions

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Hydrolycus scomberoides showing characteristic fangs and body form.

summary: The Payara, also known as the Vampire Tetra (Hydrolycus scomberoides), is a large Amazonian predator that can exceed a metre in length, recognised by elongated lower-jaw fangs that slot into pits in the upper jaw. It feeds almost exclusively on live fish and requires 1,000 litres or more with high oxygen and powerful flow. This species is suited to specialist or public-aquarium settings only.
careNotes:
The Payara is one of the most demanding large predatory characins available to the hobby. Wild fish inhabit fast-flowing, highly oxygenated stretches of the Amazon and Orinoco river systems, hunting other fish with a pair of elongated fang-like teeth on the lower jaw that can reach 6 cm in length — these teeth slot into corresponding pits in the upper jaw when the mouth is closed. The species is capable of taking prey items as large as adult piranhas.

A minimum tank volume of 1,000 litres is a firm floor, with a footprint of at least 240 cm essential for a single adult. Oxygen saturation must be maintained near the maximum for the temperature range: strong surface agitation, a powerful sump return, or dedicated powerheads directed toward the surface are all necessary. Temperature should remain in the cooler part of the tropical range — 22–26°C is preferred, as sustained temperatures above 28°C tend to cause rapid deterioration. Water chemistry should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral, reflecting Amazonian river conditions; high hardness or alkalinity is generally poorly tolerated.

The Payara is extremely skittish and prone to violent escape attempts — a completely sealed and weighted lid is non-negotiable. Specimens in captivity frequently injure themselves by striking glass at speed; black or heavily opaque side panels can help reduce startle responses. Keeping the Payara with tankmates is not generally recommended for home aquaria, as even large fish may be attacked. Trade specimens are almost always wild-caught.

The genus Hydrolycus contains four species — H. scomberoides, H. armatus (larger, with proportionally even longer fangs), H. tatauaia, and H. wallacei — that are frequently misidentified in the trade. H. armatus in particular is often sold under the Payara or Vampire Tetra name. A practical field distinction: H. scomberoides has two fang pits in the upper jaw, while H. armatus typically has one. Both species may appear in the hobby under identical common names, so confirming the species at the point of purchase is advisable.
breedingNotes: No confirmed captive breeding records exist for the Payara. The species is assumed to be a broadcast spawner in the wild — releasing eggs freely into the water column — and is thought to undertake migrations to specific spawning sites along river corridors. The space, flow rates, and seasonal water-chemistry cues that would trigger spawning cannot be replicated in home or most public aquarium settings. All trade specimens are wild-caught.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hydrolycus scomberoides
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 117 cm TL), temperature (22–27°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (2–12 dH), native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), habitat (fast-flowing channels, rapids), diet (piscivore — live fish in captivity), compatibility, and detailed husbandry notes including high-oxygen requirement, skittishness, and tank-size guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Hydrolycus scomberoides
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (117 cm TL), native range (Amazon, Orinoco, and associated drainages), taxonomy (Cynodontidae, Characiformes), diet (fish), and temperature tolerance. Confirmed species is distinct from H. armatus and H. tatauaia.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Vampire fish: Hydrolycus scomberoides
additional.notes: Supplemented husbandry details: high-flow and high-oxygen requirements, live-feeding difficulty, escape risk, genus confusion in the trade (H. armatus vs H. scomberoides), and note that fang pits distinguish the two most common trade species. Recommended tank size 1000 L+ for adults.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Payara (Vampire Tetra) Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided hobbyist-oriented care summary: temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.0–7.5, minimum 250 gallons (~950 L) for adults, carnivore/live feeder diet, expert care level, escape risk warning. Broadly consistent with SeriouslyFish data; used to cross-check tank volume lower bound and temperature range.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Payara: The Vampire Fish of the Amazon
additional.notes: Historical trade account; confirmed fang anatomy (lower-jaw teeth slot into upper-jaw pits), wild diet including piranhas, skittish behavior, and that H. armatus is frequently confused with H. scomberoides in the hobby. Noted short captive lifespan common without correct high-O2 conditions.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records exist. The Payara is assumed to be a broadcast spawner that requires migratory cues and river-corridor conditions that cannot be replicated in captivity. All trade specimens are wild-caught.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 60+ cm; needs 1000 L+ high-O2 flowing tanks and a live-fish-only diet. Specialist or public-aquarium setups only.
